name: Tiétar Valley and Casillas
name_original: Valle del Tiétar y Casillas — circular desde Sotillo de la Adrada
description: Cycle the difficult 45.7-mile Valle del Tiétar y Casillas circular from Sotillo de la Adrada for challenging climbs and scenic valley views.
region_intro: Road cycling around Sotillo De La Adrada features routes characterized by significant elevation changes and varied terrain. The region includes river valleys, such as the Tiétar Valley, and mountain passes like Puerto de Mijares. Road cyclists can expect challenging climbs and descents across the landscape. Best road cycling routes around Sotillo De La Adrada The most popular road cycling route is Tiétar Valley and Casillas, a 45.7 miles (73.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 40 minutes to complete. This difficult route features over 3,600 feet (1,100 meters) of elevation gain through the Tiétar Valley. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Puerto de Mijares – Puerto de Pedro Bernardo loop from Sotillo de la Adrada, a difficult 88.2 miles (141.9 km) path. This route includes multiple mountain passes, offering extensive climbs and panoramic views. Local road cyclists also love the Mijares – Puerto de Mijares loop from Sotillo de la Adrada, a 52.4 miles (84.3 km) trail leading through mountain passes and valleys, often completed in about 4 hours 32 minutes. Road cycling around Sotillo De La Adrada is defined by river valleys, mountain passes, and significant elevation gain. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in Sotillo De La Adrada are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 3 reviews. More than 1,200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Sotillo De La Adrada's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Sotillo De La Adrada?
A: There are over 25 road cycling routes available around Sotillo De La Adrada, catering to various skill levels from easy to difficult.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Sotillo De La Adrada?
A: Road cycling in Sotillo De La Adrada is characterized by significant elevation changes and varied terrain. You'll encounter river valleys, such as the Tiétar Valley, and challenging mountain passes like Puerto de Mijares, offering both climbs and descents.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes for beginners in Sotillo De La Adrada?
A: Yes, there are a few easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners. While the region is known for its challenging terrain, komoot lists 2 easy routes that offer a gentler introduction to road cycling here.

Q: Which routes offer significant elevation gain for experienced road cyclists?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking significant elevation gain, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24533974" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Mijares – Puerto de Pedro Bernardo loop from Sotillo de la Adrada&lt;/a&gt; stand out. This difficult 88.2-mile (141.9 km) path includes multiple mountain passes and over 9,000 feet (2,700 meters) of ascent.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Many routes in the region are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20134449" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mijares – Puerto de Mijares loop from Sotillo de la Adrada&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 52.4-mile (84.3 km) circular trail that takes you through mountain passes and valleys.

Q: What are some of the longer road cycling tours available?
A: For longer tours, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24533974" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Mijares – Puerto de Pedro Bernardo loop from Sotillo de la Adrada&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 88.2 miles (141.9 km). Another substantial option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1062312095/tietar-valley-and-casillas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tiétar Valley and Casillas&lt;/a&gt; route, covering 45.7 miles (73.5 km).

Q: What attractions can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Along your rides, you can encounter various points of interest. Notable attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2788218" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Casillas – Entrance to the Valle de Iruelas Nature Reserve&lt;/a&gt;, the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5765359" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Mosquea&lt;/a&gt;, and the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2788212" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Abuelo Chestnut Tree (Castañar de El Tiemblo)&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any natural monuments or scenic viewpoints to look out for?
A: Yes, the region offers several natural monuments and scenic spots. Keep an eye out for the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2788212" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Abuelo Chestnut Tree&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2877487" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;The Grand Chestnut of El Tiemblo&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes, especially those through mountain passes, provide panoramic views of the surrounding valleys.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Sotillo De La Adrada?
A: The road cycling routes in Sotillo De La Adrada are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 3 reviews. Cyclists often praise the challenging climbs, varied terrain, and scenic beauty of the river valleys and mountain passes.

Q: Is there a route that combines a mix of terrain and points of interest?
A: The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31888222" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cadalso de los Vidrios – Toros de Guisando loop from Sotillo de la Adrada&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 33.5-mile (53.9 km) route that offers a good mix. It features varied terrain and passes by the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2788211" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Burguillo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any less crowded road cycling options for a quieter experience?
A: While specific 'hidden gems' are not explicitly listed, exploring some of the moderate difficulty routes or those slightly off the main passes might offer a quieter experience. The region has 27 routes in total, providing ample choice beyond the most popular ones.
tips: Section of the river where you can see the Torinas Gorge and how the riverbank crosses the bridge. Depending on the part of the year you can see water in the channel or not, but it is always beautiful.

Almendral de la Cañada is a small town on the Toledo slope of the Tiétar valley surrounded by olive and almond groves. It has several bars near the road and a public fountain, ideal for a good stop on the route.

The demonym is fresnedillero. The patron saints are San Antonio de Padua, celebrated on the second Sunday in June, and the Virgen del Rosario, celebrated on the first Sunday in October. It is located in the Tiétar valley, although its lands are not irrigated by this river.
Casillas is a municipality that is located in the province of Ávila on the southern slope of the foothills of the Sierra de Gredos, at the beginning of the Tiétar valley.
Located in a nice place, next to a small lagoon (seasonal)

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This challenging road cycling route is estimated to take approximately 3 hours and 40 minutes to complete for an experienced rider.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this route?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as 'difficult' and is considered a 'Hard road ride'. It requires a very good level of fitness due to its distance of 73.5 kilometers and an elevation gain of 1,107 meters.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Valle del Tiétar y Casillas circular route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features mostly well-paved surfaces that are easy to ride. However, cyclists should be prepared for some segments that may be unpaved and difficult, including a mix of paths, streets, hiking paths, and asphalt sections.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery and views can I expect along the route?
faqs.answer: You can expect magnificent views throughout the course, traversing the picturesque Tiétar Valley. Riders will see lush forests of pines, holm oaks, chestnut trees, and oaks, as well as streams and gorges. From elevated points, there are splendid views of both the Tiétar Valley and the Iruelas Valley. On clear days, you might even spot Peña de Cenicientos, Sierra de la Higuera, Sierra del Piélago, and parts of Madrid's sierra.

faqs.question: Are there any notable points of interest or highlights along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes several interesting points. You'll encounter the Garganta de Torinas, and pass through settlements like Almendral de la Cañada, Fresnedilla, and Casillas, where you can also find the Hermitage of San Isidro (Casillas). The starting point, Sotillo de la Adrada, features traditional architecture and the recognized tourist spot 'Canto de los Pollitos'.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts and ends in Sotillo de la Adrada. While specific parking details are not provided, you can typically find parking within the town or near the main access points for trails in similar Spanish towns.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for dogs?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for race bikes on roads and paths. While dogs are generally allowed in public areas, bringing a dog on a challenging 73.5 km road cycling route is not recommended for their safety or comfort, especially given the distance and potential for mixed terrain.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Tiétar Valley experiences hot summers and cold winters. The best times for road cycling would typically be during the spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October) months when temperatures are milder and the scenery is vibrant. Avoid the peak summer heat and potential winter cold or rain.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no special permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and paths through the Valle del Tiétar.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with several other paths. Notable sections include parts of the Valle del Tiétar (14%), Sendero del Caño de la Cruz (5%), and the Cañada Real Leonesa Oriental (2%).

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or shops along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several settlements such as Almendral de la Cañada, Fresnedilla, and Casillas, as well as starting in Sotillo de la Adrada. These towns will likely offer opportunities for refreshments, cafes, or small shops to resupply.
